symptoms: Dark brown circular spots with concentric rings on older leaves
Yellowing around lesions (chlorosis)
Lesions enlarge and may merge, causing leaf drop
Stem lesions appear as dark, sunken cankers
Tomato Early Blight.cause: Fungal pathogen Alternaria solani; favored by warm humid weather (24-29°C) and leaf wetness
treatment: Remove and destroy infected lower leaves immediately
Apply Mancozeb 75WP @ 2.5 g/L water as protectant spray
Apply Azoxystrobin 23SC @ 1 mL/L water for curative action
Alternate between Mancozeb and Azoxystrobin to prevent resistance
Spray at 7-10 day intervals during disease pressure
Tomato Early Blight.organic_alternative: Copper oxychloride 50WP @ 3 g/L; Trichoderma viride @ 5 g/L as soil drench
prevention: Use certified disease-free seeds or resistant varieties (Pusa Ruby, Arka Vikas)
Maintain 60 cm row spacing for air circulation
Avoid overhead irrigation; use drip if possible
Rotate crops — avoid tomato/potato in same field for 2 years
Apply balanced NPK; excess nitrogen increases susceptibility
Tomato Early Blight.icar_reference: ICAR-IARI Technical Bulletin on Tomato Disease Management

symptoms: Water-soaked pale green or grey-green patches on leaves
White cottony mold on leaf underside in humid conditions
Lesions turn dark brown to black and spread rapidly
Fruit develops large greasy brown patches; becomes inedible
Entire plant can collapse within days under cool wet conditions
Tomato Late Blight.cause: Oomycete Phytophthora infestans; favored by cool temperatures (10-25°C) and high humidity >90%
treatment: Act immediately — Late Blight spreads extremely fast
Remove and bag infected plant parts; do NOT compost
Apply Cymoxanil + Mancozeb (Curzate M8) @ 3 g/L as first response
Follow up with Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold) @ 2 g/L
Spray every 5-7 days under high disease pressure
Ensure complete leaf coverage including undersides

pesticide.name: Cymoxanil 8% + Mancozeb 64% WP
pesticide.brand_india: Curzate M8 (Dupont/Corteva) / Blitox 50 for copper-based option
pesticide.frequency: Every 5-7 days; preventive sprays before disease onset in cool wet seasons

Tomato Late Blight.organic_alternative: Copper hydroxide 77WP @ 3 g/L; avoid in metallic sprayers
prevention: Plant resistant varieties (Arka Rakshak, Pusa Hybrid-2 in Karnataka/Maharashtra)
Apply prophylactic Bordeaux mixture (1%) spray at transplanting
Avoid planting in low-lying fields with poor drainage
Never irrigate in evening; ensure leaves dry before night
Scout fields twice weekly during monsoon / cool season
Tomato Late Blight.icar_reference: ICAR-NRC Tomato Advisory on Late Blight Management

symptoms: Irregular water-soaked lesions on leaves turning brown-black
White sporulating growth on leaf undersides in damp mornings
Dark brown lesions on stems causing collapse
Tubers develop reddish-brown rot that penetrates flesh
Infected tubers emit foul odour in storage
Potato Late Blight.cause: Phytophthora infestans; same pathogen as Tomato Late Blight but adapted to potato; spreads via wind-borne spores
treatment: Destroy haulms (vines) 10-14 days before harvest to prevent tuber infection
Apply Propamocarb + Fenamidone (Previcur Energy) @ 2 mL/L for systemic action
Mancozeb 75WP @ 2 g/L as protective spray before disease onset
Chlorothalonil 75WP @ 2 g/L for contact activity
Spray calendar: every 7 days in dry weather, every 5 days in wet/cool weather

pesticide.name: Propamocarb 66.2% + Fenamidone (or) Metalaxyl-M + Mancozeb
pesticide.dose: 2 mL or 2.5 g per litre of water depending on formulation
pesticide.frequency: Every 7 days preventively; every 5 days under disease pressure

Potato Late Blight.organic_alternative: Bordeaux mixture 1% (100g CuSO4 + 100g lime in 10L water); Trichoderma harzianum soil application
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed tubers from CPRI-certified sources
Grow resistant varieties: Kufri Girdhari, Kufri Jyoti, Kufri Bahar
Plant on well-drained ridges; avoid waterlogging
Do not store diseased tubers — sort carefully at harvest
Treat seed tubers with Mancozeb 0.2% before planting
Potato Late Blight.icar_reference: CPRI (Central Potato Research Institute) Advisory on Late Blight

symptoms: Pale greenish-yellow spots on upper leaf surface
Olive-green to brown velvety mold on leaf undersides
Affected leaves curl upward and eventually drop
Severe infections reduce photosynthesis and fruit set
Mainly affects greenhouse and protected cultivation
Tomato Leaf Mold.cause: Fungal pathogen Passalora fulva (formerly Fulvia fulva / Cladosporium fulvum); favored by high humidity (>85%) and 22-25°C
treatment: Improve ventilation in greenhouse or poly-house immediately
Remove severely infected leaves and dispose away from field
Apply Chlorothalonil 75WP @ 2 g/L for surface protection
Apply Difenoconazole 25EC @ 0.5 mL/L for systemic control
Spray every 10-14 days; avoid wetting foliage in evenings
Tomato Leaf Mold.organic_alternative: Neem oil 1500 ppm @ 3 mL/L; Bacillus subtilis-based bioagent @ 3 g/L
prevention: Use resistant varieties: Solan Lalima, Arka Abha (resistant to most races of P. fulva)
Maintain relative humidity below 80% in protected structures
Space plants adequately (60×45 cm); prune lower leaves
Avoid overhead irrigation; use drip or furrow
Sanitize greenhouse between crop cycles with 1% bleach solution
Tomato Leaf Mold.icar_reference: ICAR-IIHR Technical Bulletin on Protected Cultivation of Tomato

symptoms: Small, circular to elongated brick-red powdery pustules on both leaf surfaces
Pustules rupture to release cinnamon-brown urediniospores
Severe infection: leaves turn yellow and dry prematurely
Husks and stalks may also be affected in heavy infections
Yield loss due to reduced photosynthesis and premature senescence
Corn Common Rust.cause: Fungal pathogen Puccinia sorghi; spreads via airborne urediniospores; favored by cool temperatures (16-23°C) and dew/leaf wetness
treatment: Scout fields from V4 stage onward during cool-damp weather
Apply Propiconazole 25EC @ 1 mL/L at first appearance of pustules
Mancozeb 75WP @ 2.5 g/L as protective spray before infection
Tebuconazole 25.9EC @ 1 mL/L for systemic curative action
One to two sprays are usually sufficient if applied early

pesticide.name: Propiconazole 25% EC
pesticide.frequency: At first sign of disease; repeat in 14 days if needed

Corn Common Rust.organic_alternative: Sulfur 80WP @ 3 g/L spray; avoid above 35°C as phytotoxic
prevention: Plant resistant hybrids: DHM-117, PEHM-5, NK6240 (check local KVK recommendations)
Early planting avoids peak rust season in most zones
Balanced nutrition — avoid excess nitrogen which increases susceptibility
Crop rotation with non-grass crops reduces inoculum buildup
Destroy crop debris after harvest to reduce spore carryover
Corn Common Rust.icar_reference: ICAR-IIMR (Indian Institute of Maize Research) Advisory on Maize Diseases
